system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 4: PART IV - Frevo

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 4 — PART IV - Frevo

_Pages 104-117._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Frevo rhythmic character

> Primarily dance oriented, the frevo is basically a march, with syncopated figures in
> the melody and in the rhythmic patterns, usually up tempo.

**Significance (p. 102).** Defines the foundational rhythmic identity of frevo: syncopation and tempo context that governs all the guitar patterns.

## Frevo time signature

> Frevo is usually written in 2/4 although you can find the use of 3/4 or 4/4. The
> examples in this book are written in 4/4. The melodic accent comes on the up
> beat and the melodies are pretty much syncopated.

**Significance (p. 102).** Gives essential metric and accentuation rules for reading and performing frevo examples.

## Frevo bass and harmony style

> The
> bass part is much like a jazz “walking bass”, playing even quarter notes, and the
> harmonies are quite simple, usually turnarounds.

**Significance (p. 102).** Cross-references frevo bass to jazz walking bass and notes turnaround-based harmony, shaping how to comp.

## Right-hand pattern: syncopation placement

> Keep in your mind that the syncopations are played on
> the top voices of the chord, while the bass note comes on the beat.

**Significance (p. 104).** Core rule for basic frevo guitar pattern: prescribes which voices carry the syncopation and which carry the downbeat pulse.

## Bass note: root vs. fifth

> As in other Brazilian styles, the bass line keeps switching between the root and
> the fifth of the chord, and it's a better choice to play the fifth below the root.
> If the bass note is already placed on the 6th string, you may keep the same note
> for the whole measure.

**Significance (p. 104).** Governs left-hand voicing choices for every frevo chord, dictating the idiomatic bass motion.

## Using and combining variations

> Each variation can be played through the entire song or you can switch between
> the variations to create a new pattern. It's also common to use a different pattern
> at the end of a section or a "cadenza".

**Significance (p. 105).** Pedagogical rule explaining structural deployment of variations across a performance.

## Variation #1: new syncopation

> In this variation you'll find a new syncopation in the 3rd beat.

**Significance (p. 105).** Labels the technical difference introduced in Variation #1.

## Variation #4: up-beat only pattern

> This is a good example of a variation playing only the up beats of the measure.

**Significance (p. 108).** Defines Variation #4 as a fully up-beat texture, an important rhythmic concept in frevo accompaniment.

## Variation #4: left-hand muting technique

> You can play this example using straight 8th notes, muting the down beats with
> your left hand.

**Significance (p. 108).** Introduces a left-hand muting technique as an alternative execution for Variation #4.

## Variation #5: marcha rancho in 3/4

> This variation is an example of a “marcha rancho” in 3/4 meter.

**Significance (p. 110).** Connects Variation #5 to marcha-rancho, showing how the 3/4 feel is realised on guitar.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
